---
title: "Reproducibility Challenges: Running 3D-RCAN in 2026"
description: "Documenting the real software compatibility challenges encountered when reproducing a 2024 Nature Communications paper on modern hardware"
author: "Camila Ramirez"
date: "2026-03-15"
categories: [reproducibility, tensorflow, python, debugging]
draft: false
---

## Overview

One of the most important, and often overlooked, aspects of science is 
**reproducibility**: the ability for other researchers to run the same code 
and get the same results. This post documents the real challenges I encountered 
trying to run the 3D-RCAN pipeline from Guo et al. (2024) on modern hardware 
and software in 2026.

Spoiler: it did not work out of the box.

## My Environment

- **Computer:** MacBook Air M3 (Apple Silicon), 16GB RAM
- **OS:** macOS Sequoia 15.6
- **Python:** 3.12 (system), 3.10 (virtual environment)
- **Goal:** Run the pre-trained de-aberration model on *C. elegans* test images

## What the Code Requires

The 3D-RCAN code was written in 2021 and requires:

| Dependency | Required Version | Latest Version (2026) |
|------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Python | 3.7 | 3.13 |
| TensorFlow | 1.13 | 2.21 |
| Keras | 2.2.4 | 3.x |
| CUDA | 10.0 | 12.x |
| GPU | NVIDIA only | — |

## The Compatibility Timeline

### Attempt 1 — Run directly on Mac M3

Running `apply.py` with system Python 3.12 and TensorFlow 2.21 immediately failed:
```
AttributeError: module 'tensorflow' has no attribute 'logging'
```

**Root cause:** `tf.logging` was removed in TensorFlow 2.x.  
**Fix:** Replace with `tf.compat.v1.logging` 

### Attempt 2 — Keras serialization error

After fixing the logging issue:
```
TypeError: Could not locate class 'Model'
```

**Root cause:** Keras 3.x completely changed its model serialization format.  
**Fix attempted:** Replace `model_from_config()` with `model_from_json()` — 
partially worked but hit further issues.

### Attempt 3 — Apple Silicon threading crash
```
libc++abi: terminating due to uncaught exception of type 
std::__1::system_error: mutex lock failed
```

**Root cause:** TensorFlow's threading model conflicts with macOS on M3.  
**Fix:** Set `OBJC_DISABLE_INITIALIZE_FORK_SAFETY=YES` 

### Attempt 4 — Google Colab

Moved to Google Colab hoping their NVIDIA GPUs would help:
```
ERROR: No matching distribution found for tensorflow-gpu==1.13.1
```

**Root cause:** TensorFlow 1.x is no longer available on any platform.

### Attempt 5 — Python 3.10 + tensorflow-macos

Created a fresh virtual environment with Python 3.10 and `tensorflow-macos==2.12.0` 
(the last version before Keras 3 broke everything):
```
Metal device set to: Apple M3
systemMemory: 16.00 GB
Loading model from RCAN_model/weights_169_0.05605552.hdf5
```

Progress! The model file was found and Metal GPU detected. But then:
```
ValueError: bad marshal data (unknown type code)
```

**Root cause:** The model contains Lambda layers whose bytecode was compiled 
with Python 3.7. Python's `marshal` module is not compatible across versions, 
so the bytecode cannot be loaded in Python 3.10.

## Why This Matters

This experience highlights **software rot**, a real and growing problem in 
computational science. Code that worked perfectly in 2021 may not run at all 
in 2026 due to:

- Dependency deprecation
- Breaking API changes between major versions  
- Hardware architecture evolution (x86 → Apple Silicon)
- Cloud platform updates removing old package versions

## Next Steps

1. Continue working with the original authors to get a working environment
2. Rewrite the inference code in modern PyTorch which runs natively on Mac M3
3. Compare results to the paper's published figures
